title: STANDARD FOR DAIRY FAT SPREADS CXS 253-2006 Adopted in 2006. Amended in 2008, 2010, 2018, 2022, 2023.

sections.section: STANDARD FOR DAIRY FAT SPREADS CXS 253-2006 Adopted in 2006. Amended in 2008, 2010, 2018, 2022, 2023.
sections.summary: Title and identification of the Codex Standard for Dairy Fat Spreads (CXS 253‑2006), noting adoption in 2006 and amendments in 2008, 2010, 2018, 2022 and 2023.
Notice that, following the Forty‑fifth Session of the Codex Alimentarius Commission (Dec 2022), amendments were made to Section 7.3 on labelling of non‑retail containers.

Notice that, following the Forty‑sixth Session of the Codex Alimentarius Commission (Dec 2023), the standard's food‑additive provisions were amended and aligned with the General Standard for Food Additives (GSFA, CXS 192‑1995).
Scope: this standard applies to dairy fat spreads intended as spreads for direct consumption or for further processing, in accordance with Section 2 of the standard.
Description: dairy fat spreads are milk‑derived products rich in fat forming a spreadable water‑in‑milk‑fat emulsion that remains solid at 20 °C.

Essential composition and quality factors: permitted raw materials include milk and milk products (which may be processed), permitted ingredients include flavours, processing aids, specified nutrients (subject to national legislation), salts, sugars, inulin, maltodextrins, starter cultures, water, gelatine and starches (use limited by GMP), and the milk fat content must be ≥10% and <80% (m/m) and represent at least two‑thirds of dry matter, with compositional changes subject to the General Standard for the Use of Dairy Terms.
Raw materials: specifies milk and/or products obtained from milk as raw materials and permits prior appropriate processing (e.g., physical modifications including fractionation) of these materials.

Permitted ingredients: lists allowed additions—flavours, processing aids, permitted nutrients (per national legislation), sodium/potassium chloride, sugars, inulin, maltodextrins, harmless starter cultures, water, gelatine and starches—subject to GMP and limited use only as functionally necessary.

Composition: milk fat must be at least 10% but less than 80% (m/m) and must constitute at least two‑thirds of the product's dry matter, with compositional modifications constrained by the General Standard for the Use of Dairy Terms (CXS 206‑1999).

Food additives: only additive functional classes deemed technologically justified per the GSFA (CXS 192‑1995) for food category 02.2.2 may be used, with permitted classes and their acceptability depending on milk‑fat content (<70% versus ≥70%) and application limited by GMP (noting certain classes are not justified for high‑fat products).

Products covered by this standard must comply with the maximum contaminant and toxin levels specified in the General Standard for Contaminants and Toxins in Food and Feed (CXS 193-1995); milk used in manufacture must also meet the contaminant/toxin limits in CXS 193-1995 and Codex maximum residue limits for veterinary drugs and pesticides in milk.

Recommends that products be prepared and handled according to the General Principles of Food Hygiene (CXC 1-1969), the Code of Hygienic Practice for Milk and Milk Products (CXC 57-2004) and other relevant Codex texts, and comply with any microbiological criteria established per CXG 21-1997.

In addition to the General Standard for the Labelling of Pre-packaged Foods (CXS 1-1985) and the General Standard for the Use of Dairy Terms (CXS 206-1999), this section imposes specific labelling provisions for dairy fat spreads covering name of the food, declaration of fat content, and labelling of non-retail containers (see 7.1–7.3).

Sets specific labelling rules for 'Dairy fat spread': the name shall be 'Dairy fat spread' (other names allowed if national law permits), 'reduced fat' claims may be used per CXG 23-1997, translations must be non‑misleading, salted/unsalted status may be indicated per national law, and sweetened spreads must be labelled as such.
The milk fat content must be declared in a manner acceptable in the country of retail sale either as a percentage by mass or as grams per serving (with the number of servings stated).
Labelling of non-retail containers should follow the General Standard for the Labelling of Non‑Retail Containers of Foods (CXS 346-2021).
Compliance checking must use the methods of analysis and sampling contained in the Recommended Methods of Analysis and Sampling (CXS 234-1999) that are relevant to this standard's provisions.
